interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Baptist Minister; SCLC Executive Director (1960-1964); Chief of Staff to Martin Luther King Jr.
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture / Library of Congress

main_summary.summary:
Reverend Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker recounts his life as a Baptist minister, SCLC strategist, and self-described "chief of staff" to Martin Luther King Jr. Born in Brockton, Massachusetts on August 16, 1928, Walker grew up the son of a scholarly preacher father in a section of South Jersey so segregated that the family called it "Little Georgia." He describes losing his first childhood playmate, Nancy, who told him she could no longer play with him because he was "colored," and a portrait of Frederick Douglass on the wall of his boyhood home. After studying physics and chemistry at Virginia Union University and earning his MDiv at its graduate school of religion, he was called at age 22 (in fact 23, he says, after discovering his actual birth year was 1928) to Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg, Virginia, where he served eight years and became known as a "race man" who initially carried a Smith & Wesson nickel-plated .32 before adopting King's nonviolent commitment.

Walker organized the integration of the Petersburg ministerial alliance and on February 27, 1960 led a sit-in at the segregated Petersburg public library, where he was arrested after requesting volume one of Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee. He describes the action as the first library desegregation in the South. He joined SCLC at a Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting (he believes in 1958), and was named Executive Director in 1960, succeeding Ella Baker as the organization's interim executive director. Walker took SCLC from a $57,000 budget and an idea "in Dr. King's briefcase" to a $1,000,000 budget and one hundred full-time employees over four years, recruiting two staff from Petersburg. He frames Albany, Georgia (which he reached after SNCC organizers invited King) as a misperceived success that taught the movement how to mobilize an entire community.

Walker authored Project C ("Confrontation"), the year-in-advance Birmingham campaign blueprint -- he personally walked downtown stores measuring travel times and counting lunch-counter seats. He recounts how Bull Connor's reactions, including fire hoses turned on children during the Children's Crusade (planned with King and James Bevel), produced the televised images on the Huntley-Brinkley report that turned national opinion. Walker says he was the only person in Birmingham who could read King's "chicken-scratch" handwriting, transcribing the Letter from Birmingham Jail from newspaper margins and toilet paper with his secretary, and naming the document after the Quakers (Religious Society of Friends) first printed it as "tears of love." He then served as Southern Regional Coordinator for the 1963 March on Washington. Walker left SCLC for a $25,000 publishing job in New York after the SCLC board froze his salary at $10,000, despite Daddy King (Martin Luther King Sr.) personally visiting his Atlanta home to ask him to stay. He pastored Canaan Baptist Church in Harlem for 37 years, built it to 1,800 members, became the largest developer of affordable housing in New York State, was active in the charter-school movement (opening the first charter school in New York with Steve Klinsky and allying with Rev. Floyd Flake and state senator Ruben Diaz Sr.), and was recruited into the U.S. anti-apartheid movement by African National Congress representatives, taking 22 ministers to South Africa and participating in the election that brought Nelson Mandela to the presidency.

Walker's wife Theresa (present at the interview) provides a long testimony about Mother's Day 1963: after A.D. King's home was bombed in Birmingham and her husband stayed to quiet the rioting community, an Alabama National Guardsman struck her in the head at the Gaston Motel; she was later arrested in East Point, Georgia with her five children (ages 6, 8, 9, 10, and 11), and Robert J. Brown paid her $84 fine. Walker himself was beaten the same Mother's Day weekend, sustaining a broken wrist, three broken ribs, and a dislocated back from Alabama state highway patrolmen's batons. The interview closes with reflections on the religious infrastructure of the Black church as the movement's foundation, King's debt to Matthew, Mark, Luke and John (per Andrew Young), and Walker's mentor Vernon Johns's dictum that "the truth is always inflammatory." Walker also mentions that Maya Angelou ran his fundraising office on 25th Street in New York and wrote about meeting him in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings.
key_themes: Origins of SCLC leadership and organizational strategy
Project C and the Birmingham campaign as turning point
Religious infrastructure of the Black freedom movement
Nonviolence as adopted discipline rather than instinct
Personal and family cost of movement work
Continuity of antiracist struggle from U.S. South to South African apartheid
main_summary.historical_significance: Walker's testimony is among the most direct surviving insider accounts of SCLC's organizational rise and the architecture of the 1963 Birmingham campaign, since he personally built the Project C blueprint, ran the year-long reconnaissance, and transcribed King's Letter from Birmingham Jail. The interview also documents the February 27, 1960 Petersburg library sit-in as a first-in-the-South integration action, preserves Theresa Walker's firsthand account of the May 1963 Birmingham riot and East Point arrest, and traces a single ministerial life across SCLC, the March on Washington, a thirty-seven-year Harlem pastorate, charter-school activism, and the U.S. anti-apartheid mobilization that aided Mandela's election. It corrects the public framing of Albany as a failure and supplies an SCLC-internal accounting of the fundraising and staff scale-up that made the movement nationally consequential.

errors: Walker himself misstates his Petersburg arrival age. He says he was "called to a church in Petersburg in my last year" at age 22 (then corrects himself to 23) and says he served Gillfield Baptist Church eight years before going to New York; the corpus dates his Gillfield pastorate as 1953-1958. The summary repeats Walker's framing of the chronology rather than reconciling it with the corpus, which is a minor risk of internal inconsistency.

Walker says he joined SCLC at the Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting "in 1958, I think it was," but elsewhere says he became Executive Director in 1960. The summary repeats both years as Walker stated them; the transcript is the source for both. Treated as faithful to transcript even where Walker's own memory hedges.

Summary uses canonical name "Douglas Southall Freeman" while transcript renders it "Douglas Elvall Freeman." Cross-referenced against ground-truth common knowledge of the canonical Lee biography; treated as Whisper rendering of the same name and supported.
improvements: Could quote Walker's own phrasing of Project C more directly ("Project C confrontation that we had to confront the evil that we discerned in the segregation system").
Could add explicit attribution of Theresa Walker's testimony as her own voice rather than narrating it as Walker's account.
Could note that Walker's claim of 55 written and 30 published books is his self-report, not independently verified.

chapters.title: Childhood in 'Little Georgia,' New Jersey and Call to Ministry
chapters.summary: Walker introduces himself as a Brockton, Massachusetts native born August 16, 1928, the son of a Baptist preacher who served a hundred-member congregation in South Jersey and read Greek and Hebrew daily. He describes losing his childhood playmate Nancy after she told him she could no longer play with him because he was "colored," attending segregated elementary schools before integration in junior high and high school, witnessing the Ku Klux Klan burn a cross on a Father Divine headquarters, and growing up beneath a portrait of Frederick Douglass. He calls his father a "race man" who shaped his lifelong stance, and explains that although he majored in physics and chemistry intending to be a physician, his Richmond, Virginia experiences with segregation moved him toward ministry as "the best means of fighting segregation." He calls the Black preacher "the freest man in society" because accountable only to the Black community.

improvements: Could note that the cross-burning at "Father Divine" headquarters is Walker's specific recollection of a single incident rather than a generic Klan reference.

chapters.title: Harlem Pastorate, Petersburg, and Ministerial Alliance Integration
chapters.summary: Walker traces his pastoral career: graduate work at Virginia Union, his eight-year pastorate at Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg beginning at age 22 (which he later discovered was actually 23), corporate marketing director work at a New York publishing firm, then a 37-year pastorate at a Harlem church. He describes leading the integration of the segregated Petersburg ministerial alliance with the chaplain of Virginia State and another local pastor, R.G. Williams, and -- much later in his Harlem ministry -- becoming the largest developer of affordable housing in New York State and organizing against the drug trade.

errors: Walker's self-reported birth year revision (1928 instead of 1929) and corresponding age at Petersburg (23 instead of 22) is hedged in the transcript and faithfully retained, but readers should note the chronological tension.
improvements: Could specify that Gillfield Baptist Church is identified by Walker as "over 200 years old."

chapters.title: Petersburg Library Sit-In, February 27, 1960
chapters.summary: Walker describes attempting to get the Petersburg city council to integrate the public library and being refused. When he believed the NAACP would not pursue a court case, he says he "influenced the gang by King" and organized a demonstration on February 27, 1960. He and R.G. Williams entered through the "white door," were arrested within minutes, and chose to stay in jail for two days. Walker says it was the first library desegregation in the South. He recounts requesting volume one of Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee, choosing the book ironically because he "always thought Lee was guilty of treason" and wanted to understand the Southern view that made Lee a hero.

improvements: Walker's claim that it was the first Southern library desegregation could be cross-referenced; ground-truth corpus confirms the Petersburg library action was canonical, but the "first" framing is Walker's own.

chapters.title: Meeting King, Putting Down the Gun, and Earlier Violent Fantasies
chapters.summary: Walker says he met King in approximately 1952 and that King put him on his board and assigned him press-release writing. He says the day before King's assassination, King had called his home wanting to talk, and Walker has always imagined King wanted his help with the Memphis march that had gone wrong. He recounts going to Petersburg armed with a Smith & Wesson nickel-plated .32 and, under King's influence, putting the gun down. He traces this defensive impulse to his father, who once concealed his hand on a gun trigger inside a coat as a constable approached him with a nightstick in a segregated Norfolk-area train station waiting room. Walker also reveals that during college he had fantasized about recruiting young Black men to use plastic bombs against Senators Theodore Bilbo and James Eastland -- before becoming King's nonviolent "right-hand man."

errors: Walker's date of meeting King in 1952 is hedged in the transcript ("It must have been 1952") and is earlier than commonly documented; the summary retains Walker's own framing.
improvements: Could explicitly mark the plastic-bomb fantasy as Walker's own framing of his pre-nonviolent thinking, lest readers misinterpret as plan or action.

chapters.title: Joining SCLC and Building the Organization
chapters.summary: Walker recounts that King invited him to SCLC's initial Atlanta meeting but he could not afford the trip. He joined at the Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting in 1958 ("I think it was"), came on staff in 1960 as Executive Director after Ella Baker's interim tenure, and built SCLC from "mainly an idea in Dr. King's briefcase" with a $57,000 first-year budget to a million-dollar budget and 100 full-time staff in four years. He brought two members from his Petersburg staff. He frames himself primarily as administrator and fundraiser, and identifies Albany and Birmingham as the two campaigns where things began "popping." He calls money "the ammunition" of the movement.

errors: Walker's 1958 Clarksdale date is hedged. Standard scholarship places SCLC's founding in 1957 in Atlanta. The summary preserves Walker's hedged 1958 because the transcript is the source of truth here.
improvements: Could specify that Walker's 100-employee figure is at the four-year mark, which the transcript establishes explicitly.

chapters.title: Albany, SNCC, and the Charles Sherrod Connection
chapters.summary: Walker rejects the consensus view that Albany was a failure, calling it a success with mistakes. He says Albany had "too many targets" and was initiated by SNCC organizers (the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee), which exhausted the community on rallies and fundraising before King arrived. When SNCC realized they were "in over their heads" they invited King, and Walker followed. He recounts intra-movement tension among CORE, the NAACP, the local community organization, and SCLC, and pushes back on journalist Louis Lomax's reporting -- noting Lomax never came to Albany. He says when SCLC left Albany, the parks and library were closed; the chief significance was learning to mobilize an entire community, without which Birmingham would not have been won. He identifies Charles (Melvin) Sherrod, the SNCC organizer in Albany, as someone he "raised in my congregation in Petersburg" and helped finance through college via a Woolworth grant.

improvements: Could specify Walker's anecdote about the SCLC-SNCC vote ("contrary to his advice, I thought was a mistake") more concretely.

chapters.title: Project C: The Birmingham Campaign Blueprint
chapters.summary: Walker describes designing Project C ("Confrontation") at King's assignment, going to Birmingham a year in advance to measure travel times for young and middle-aged people to downtown stores and to count lunch-counter seats. The SCLC executive committee accepted the plan and the name unanimously. He explains that Bull Connor played "right into our hands" with fire hoses and police dogs because Connor had decided the marches should not be allowed downtown -- whereas if Connor had simply let three or four marches pass uneventfully, the campaign would have lost its drama. Walker, King, and James Bevel decided to use children in the marches despite anticipated criticism, citing the precedent that singer Al Hibbler had been arrested in Birmingham; the Children's Crusade is what "toppled the segregation system" because images of firemen turning hoses on children running down sidewalks became unacceptable to the nation. Walker recounts calling King from a phone after a late-starting march that drew a thousand onlookers, telling him he had figured out a tactic: slowing down the demonstrations until Bull Connor reacted, then making the Huntley-Brinkley report two or three nights running. He says the NAACP was outlawed in Alabama and relied on courts, which would have taken 100 years.

improvements: Could specify the May 1963 dates of the Children's Crusade more explicitly; the transcript does not give the dates and Walker is generalizing.

chapters.title: Personal Cost, Civil Rights Acts, and Letter from Birmingham Jail
chapters.summary: Walker says he never thought about personal danger because of his religious fervor. He notes that his wife was assaulted by an Alabama State Trooper with a rifle and that he himself was beaten on Mother's Day, sustaining a broken wrist, three broken ribs, and a dislocated back from Alabama State Highway Patrolmen's batons. He calls Birmingham euphoric and credits Birmingham with producing the Public Accommodations Act of 1964 and -- through the Selma March -- the Voting Rights Act of 1965. He was named Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington. He describes transcribing the Letter from Birmingham Jail from edges of newspapers and toilet paper as the only person who could read King's "chicken-scratch" handwriting, working with his secretary across two or three nights. He calls the Letter "the most important document of the 20th century" and says it "equals the Gettysburg address." He notes the Religious Society of Friends (the Quakers) printed the first version, calling it "tears of love," and that he himself proposed the title "Letter from the Birmingham Jail." He emphasizes that King wrote it from memory without books or library, "muscle memory from his training."

errors: Summary describes the 1964 Act as the "Public Accommodations Act of 1964," which is Walker's framing of Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964; the more standard reference is the full Civil Rights Act. Treated as faithful to Walker's terminology.
improvements: Could explicitly note Walker's hyperbole ("equals the Gettysburg address") as Walker's own claim rather than the summary's assertion.

chapters.title: Leaving SCLC for New York: Salary, Daddy King, and Charter Schools
chapters.summary: Walker says he left SCLC because the board froze his salary at $10,000 while he administered a million-dollar budget and supported a wife and four children. A New York publisher offered $25,000 to be marketing vice president for a book series on Black life in America. He recounts Martin Luther King Sr. ("Daddy King") visiting his Atlanta home and begging him to stay, looking out at the stream running through his property. Walker remained King's New York representative including to the United Nations, and went to jail with King in 1967 in Birmingham for parading without a permit; he says they took each other's photographs there and that he holds the rights to the famous jail photo. He pastored a Harlem church (Canaan Baptist) for 37 years, growing it to 1,800 members with 80% tithing and over a million dollars in member contributions in his final two years. He became central to New York's charter-school movement, opening the state's first charter school with Steve Klinsky and partnering with state senator Ruben Diaz Sr. and Rev. Floyd Flake to win charter-school legislation in New York.

improvements: Could note that Walker's claim of opening "the first charter school in New York" is his own assertion; verifying which charter school is first by date is outside transcript scope.

chapters.title: Anti-Apartheid Activism, Theology, and 'No Post-Racial Era'
chapters.summary: Walker says he was recruited into the U.S. anti-apartheid movement by African National Congress representatives he identifies as John Stone and Mark Adini, and that he mobilized churches into a 300-member federation, traveled to South Africa several times (once with 22 ministers), and "actually worked in the election of Mandela becoming president." He describes apartheid as systematized in "the reformed church" and condemns it as a terrible form of racism that denied indigenous South Africans land ownership and required pass books. Asked about the theological foundation of his politics, he grounds it in the New Testament teachings of Jesus, and recounts that Andrew Young told an author writing on "the making of a mind" about Dr. King that whereas the author had been told King was influenced by Martin Buber, Henry David Thoreau, and Paul Tillich, Young insisted King "was a Christian pastor" influenced "by Matthew, Mark, Luke and John." Walker also names Vernon Johns and Gardner C. Taylor as articulators of the beloved community. He rejects the post-Obama framing of a "post-racial era," calling residual racism unchanged for many.

errors: ANC contact names "John Stone" and "Mark Adini" are Walker's stated recollection. The transcript renders them as "John Stone" and "Mark Adini." These could be Whisper renderings of other ANC figures; without external corroboration the summary preserves Walker's naming as transcribed.
improvements: Could explicitly mark the ANC contact names as uncertain ASR renderings.

chapters.title: Memphis Reflections, Theresa Walker's Mother's Day 1963 Testimony, and Family Cost
chapters.summary: Walker briefly addresses Memphis, attributing the disrupted Memphis march to a group he names as "the invaders" who he believes were "put up to it by the FBI," framing federal disruption of the movement -- particularly under the Kennedy administration -- as a recurring pattern. Theresa Walker then provides extended testimony. On the Saturday before Mother's Day 1963, her husband was supposed to come home but stayed in Birmingham at King's request because "someone had bombed a King, Dr. King's brother's home," causing community riots. She brought her four children to Birmingham. While Walker was out attempting to quiet the crowd, Alabama National Guardsmen ordered her into the lobby of the Gaston Motel; when she refused to leave without her two sleeping children, a Guardsman struck her in the head and sent her to the hospital. The next day she drove back to Atlanta, was pulled over by an East Point, Georgia police officer two blocks from the Atlanta airport, and was arrested with her five children (ages 11, 10, 9, 8, and 6). She used her phone call to reach Martin Luther King Sr. via Andrew Young's wife Jean Childs Young. Daddy King arrived around midnight with a property owner who posted bail (Georgia required outright property ownership without a mortgage). At her later trial she was fined $84, refused to pay, and was re-jailed until Robert J. Brown -- traveling with her husband from Atlanta -- paid the $84 fine. Walker himself was simultaneously beaten by three Alabama state highway patrolmen at his motel for his belongings, with broken wrist, three fractured ribs, and dislocated spine. Theresa Walker also served as a Mississippi freedom rider, sharing a two-person cell with 14 other women. Walker says he has written 55 books, 30 of them published, with 30 more to come. Theresa Walker adds that during their Petersburg years, when Walker was president of the Petersburg Improvement Association, of CORE, and of SCLC, their daughter was barred from all public schools after they refused to sign a pupil-placement form pledging not to send her to a white school; she homeschooled the daughter with lesson plans from church teachers, and the daughter went on to finish Howard University in three years, complete a master's at Yale, and become deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts.

errors: Theresa Walker says "they arrested my 11-year-old, 10-year-old, the nine-year-old, eight-year-old, and the six-year-old" -- five children. Earlier in her testimony she says "my husband couldn't come home, so he asked me to bring the four children to Birmingham." The summary notes both numbers as the transcript states them and does not reconcile them; the discrepancy belongs to the transcript itself.
improvements: Could explicitly flag the four-children-vs-five-children arithmetic tension as an artifact of the transcript rather than the summary.

chapters.title: Closing Reflections: Vernon Johns, Maya Angelou, and Truth
chapters.summary: Walker says he is not bitter and would not have done anything differently. Asked about regret, he says the 1960s was an exciting time and that others gave more, including their lives. He cites his mentor Vernon Johns's dictum that "the truth is always inflammatory" as his own guiding principle. He also recounts that Maya Angelou worked on his staff, running his New York fundraising office on 25th Street, and that she wrote about meeting him with King in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings -- including the line that he was "too young for wisdom and too handsome for virtue."
related_events: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings

improvements: Could specify that the quoted Maya Angelou line is Walker quoting Angelou from memory and may not be verbatim from the book.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Baptist Minister; SCLC Executive Director 1960-1964; Chief of Staff to Martin Luther King Jr.
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture / Library of Congress

evidence: Walker frames his entire life around his SCLC chief-of-staff identity from the opening: 'my only claim to fame is that I was once chief of staff at Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.' (Segment 5, 00:00:44,000)

The chronological flow from Brockton birth to Petersburg ministry to SCLC to Harlem to anti-apartheid is logical and the interviewer maintains topic anchors. (Segments 2-477, 00:00:08,000 to 00:52:07,000)
Theresa Walker's Mother's Day testimony is a self-contained, well-structured narrative within the broader arc. (Segments 585-613, 00:42:43,000 to 01:06:50,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Strong overall organization moving chronologically from childhood through SCLC tenure to later activism, with clear topic transitions guided by the interviewer. Some backtracking on dates (1958 vs 1960, age 22 vs 23) and a late mid-interview pivot to Theresa Walker's testimony create minor friction, but the listener never loses thread.

most_vivid_moment.quote: I always thought Lee was guilty of treason. And I just thought I'd look at this book to see what the Southern view was. Why they made him such a hero.

evidence: 'I asked for a volume one of Douglas Elvall [Southall] Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee' (Segment 88, 00:10:30,000)
'I measured how long it would take a young person, a middle-aged person to go to the stores. How many seats were available in the eating facility' (Segments 238-240, 00:26:58,000)
'My father said he had his coat on and his trigger on the gun. And he said, by the time it started down. He was going to shoot him.' (Segments 118-120, 00:13:18,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Exceptional concrete detail: specific dates (February 27, 1960; August 16, 1928), full names (R.G. Williams, Charles Sherrod, Bull Connor, Steve Klinsky, Robert J. Brown, Ruben Diaz Sr., Floyd Flake, Vernon Johns), specific addresses (25th Street fundraising office), measurable activities (Project C reconnaissance with counted seats), and dramatic reconstructions (the father's hidden trigger; the playmate Nancy's tearful refusal).

most_authentic_moment.quote: I lost my first girlfriend because of it. It was a little girl who lived within two blocks from my home, named Nancy. And we used to play together. And one day she came to the curb from her house and she was sort of crying. And she said she couldn't play with me anymore. I said why? She said because you're colored. I didn't even know what that meant.

vulnerability_elements: Admits carrying a gun before adopting nonviolence
Admits college fantasy of plastic-bomb attacks on Bilbo and Eastland
Admits leaving SCLC for higher pay and explicitly resenting Black employer exploitation
Admits to past bitterness now resolved
Shares physical injuries (broken wrist, ribs, spine) with detachment
emotional_authenticity.justification: Walker is generally guarded and ministerial in tone but offers several deeply vulnerable disclosures: Nancy the playmate, the plastic-bomb fantasy, the financial humiliation by the SCLC board. Theresa Walker's account adds raw family-level detail. Loses points because Walker himself stays largely composed and analytical in places where deeper feeling could surface.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: A masterful storyteller with a long lifetime of practiced retelling, Walker delivers a tightly organized, rich-with-specifics narrative anchored in named persons and dated events. He is more vulnerable than the typical institutional figure of his era, especially in disclosing the gun-carrying, the bomb fantasy, and the salary dispute. Theresa Walker's intervention sharply elevates the emotional and narrative texture in the final third. The combination of professional polish and occasional unguarded reflection makes this an unusually rich oral-history hour and thirteen minutes.

major_events: Petersburg Public Library sit-in February 27, 1960 (Segments 77-85, 00:09:32,000) - principal organizer and arrestee
Birmingham campaign 1963 / Project C (Segments 236-302, 00:26:36,000) - chief architect
Children's Crusade May 1963 (Segments 261-275, 00:29:26,000) - decision-maker alongside King and Bevel
Letter from Birmingham Jail transcription April 1963 (Segments 374-402, 00:41:12,000) - principal transcriber and namer
March on Washington August 1963 (Segments 330-332, 00:35:54,000) - Southern Regional Coordinator

key_figures.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Chief of staff and SCLC Executive Director under King

key_figures.name: Martin Luther King Sr. (Daddy King)
key_figures.relationship: Personal mentor; pleaded with Walker to stay at SCLC; later coordinated Theresa's bail

key_figures.name: Charles Sherrod
key_figures.relationship: Raised in Walker's Petersburg congregation; SNCC organizer in Albany

key_figures.name: Ella Baker
key_figures.relationship: SCLC interim executive director whom Walker succeeded

key_figures.name: Andrew Young
key_figures.relationship: SCLC colleague; Walker's wife called Young's wife Jean Childs Young during East Point arrest

key_figures.name: Robert J. Brown
key_figures.relationship: Drove from Atlanta with Walker after Theresa's arrest; paid her $84 fine

key_figures.name: Maya Angelou
key_figures.relationship: Worked on Walker's staff running New York fundraising office on 25th Street

key_figures.name: Nelson Mandela
key_figures.relationship: Walker says he worked in the election that brought Mandela to the presidency

key_figures.name: Vernon Johns
key_figures.relationship: Mentor; source of Walker's 'truth is always inflammatory' dictum

organizational_involvement.role: Co-founder of first NY charter school

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Walker had unique access to the operational interior of SCLC: the salary politics of the board, the day-to-day budget administration, the year-long Birmingham reconnaissance methodology, the typing-from-toilet-paper transcription of the Letter from Birmingham Jail, the back-channel calls between King and field staff, and the post-King interpretation by Andrew Young of King's theological influences. He also possesses unique insider knowledge of the Petersburg library action as 'the first library desegregation in the South' and of the SCLC-SNCC tensions in Albany.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Walker was one of the closest operational figures in SCLC and the principal architect of the most consequential SCLC campaign. Few surviving witnesses had this combination of strategic role, personal closeness to King, and longevity into late life. His specific recollections of mechanics (Project C measurements; the Letter transcription; the salary freeze) are exactly the kind of insider information that would not appear in published histories without his testimony.

precision_examples: $57,000 first-year SCLC budget growing to $1,000,000 with 100 full-time staff over four years (Segments 153-157, 00:17:17,000)
Salary frozen at $10,000 vs publisher offer of $25,000 (Segments 406-417, 00:44:11,000)
$84 East Point fine paid by Robert J. Brown (Segments 609-612, 01:05:58,000)
1,800-member Harlem congregation with 80% tithing and >$1 million in tithes in final two years (Segments 443-444, 00:47:44,000)
verifiable_claims: February 27, 1960 Petersburg library sit-in (verifiable in civil rights chronology)
Walker as SCLC Executive Director 1960-1964 (verifiable; corpus-confirmed)
Walker as principal transcriber of Letter from Birmingham Jail (verifiable in King historiography)
Southern Regional Coordinator role for March on Washington (verifiable)
A.D. King home bombing on May 11, 1963 (corroborated by ground-truth corpus)
Bull Connor as Birmingham Commissioner of Public Safety (corpus-confirmed)
specificity_of_details.justification: Walker provides verifiable numerical details (budgets, staff size, fines, ages, book counts) that distinguish this interview from oral histories that traffic in vague generalities. The few hedged dates ("1952" for first meeting with King; "1958, I think" for Clarksdale) are explicitly flagged by Walker himself.

new_information: Operational detail of Project C reconnaissance (measuring travel times, counting seats)
The author identification (Walker) of the phrase 'Letter from the Birmingham Jail' as a title, replacing the Quakers' first-published 'tears of love'
Theresa Walker's first-person East Point Georgia arrest narrative with her five children
Walker's plastic-bomb-against-Bilbo-and-Eastland college fantasy as personal pre-history of his nonviolent conversion
Andrew Young's reported correction of intellectual-biography framing of King ('Matthew, Mark, Luke and John')
Walker's role in opening the first New York charter school with Steve Klinsky
understudied_aspects: SCLC's internal organizational economics (budget growth, salary politics)
The role of Black church federations in 1970s-90s anti-apartheid mobilization
Family-level impact on movement leaders' wives and children (Theresa's testimony; daughter barred from public schools)
Walker's late-life charter-school activism as continuation of movement work
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Walker's defense of Albany as a success rather than failure complicates the consensus reading of the Albany Movement as an SCLC defeat. His criticism of the Kennedy administration as actively obstructive (channeling movement energy into voter registration to defang direct action) complicates the more sympathetic public memory of the Kennedys on civil rights. His framing of 'the invaders' in Memphis as 'put up to it by the FBI' adds an unverifiable but historically grounded line of FBI-disruption interpretation.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of SCLC organizational development, Birmingham campaign planning, the production history of the Letter from Birmingham Jail, the gendered burdens of movement family life, and the post-1960s continuity of Black-church-based activism into anti-apartheid and charter-school politics would draw heavily on this interview.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: The interview supplies substantial behind-the-scenes detail on SCLC mechanics, the Birmingham campaign blueprint, and the Letter from Birmingham Jail's production that complement rather than duplicate published scholarship. It also documents an under-narrated post-1968 chapter of Black ministerial activism into anti-apartheid and charter-school politics.

historical_value.overall_assessment: An exceptionally high-value historical document. Walker speaks from positions inside SCLC's headquarters that very few other witnesses could occupy, and his recollections are textured with verifiable specifics. The interview is particularly valuable for the operational history of the Birmingham campaign, the production history of the Letter from Birmingham Jail, and the often-invisible family cost of movement leadership (rendered concretely by Theresa Walker). The only ceiling on the score is that some of Walker's most dramatic framings (Albany as success; FBI infiltration in Memphis; the Letter as 'equal to the Gettysburg address') are his interpretive claims rather than verifiable facts.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Walker traces an arc from armed self-defense (Smith & Wesson .32) and college-era plastic-bomb fantasies against Bilbo and Eastland to becoming King's principal nonviolent organizer; from a Petersburg pastor who initially considered medicine to the SCLC Executive Director who built the organization to a million-dollar budget; from southern movement leader to Harlem pastor, anti-apartheid organizer, charter-school activist, and elder reconciled with a once-bitter past.
key_turning_points: Nancy refusing to play (Segment 17, 00:02:03,000) - first racial consciousness
Father's hidden gun trigger in train station (Segments 118-120, 00:13:23,000) - inherited posture
Reading King and adopting nonviolence (Segment 127, 00:13:54,000) - conversion
February 27, 1960 library sit-in (Segment 77, 00:09:32,000) - first direct action
Salary freeze and departure to New York publisher (Segments 406-422, 00:44:11,000) - exit from SCLC
Recruitment by ANC representatives (Segments 471-477, 00:51:11,000) - second-life activism
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: armed self-defender, would-be physician, fantasizing plastic-bomb retaliation. After: nonviolent strategist, ministerial elder, anti-apartheid organizer, no longer bitter.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Yes - the gun-to-nonviolence conversion narrative, the late-life pivot to anti-apartheid and education work, and the closing dictum that 'evil is persistent and you have to fight it persistently' offer a model of sustained, recommitted activism across more than half a century.
transformation_growth.justification: Walker explicitly narrates two major transformations: the gun-down conversion to nonviolence under King's influence, and the late-life movement from bitter to reconciled. The arc is well-articulated and feels earned through specific incidents rather than abstract claims.

peak_moments.description: Library sit-in arrest and two-day jail stay
peak_moments.stakes: First Southern library desegregation; possible extended jailing

peak_moments.description: Phone call to King figuring out the slowdown tactic
Mother's Day 1963 beating and Theresa's National Guard assault
peak_moments.stakes: Physical injury to Walker; head trauma to wife; arrest of all five children
Producing the canonical movement document under time pressure

peak_moments.description: Daddy King's plea to stay at SCLC
peak_moments.stakes: Walker's choice between movement loyalty and family economic security

conflict_types: Intra-movement organizational competition (SCLC vs SNCC, NAACP, CORE)
Federal government as adversary (FBI, Kennedy administration)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From childhood loss of friendship through pre-movement rage to organizational triumph in Birmingham, personal injury on Mother's Day, painful departure from SCLC, and late-life equilibrium.
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: The interview presents multiple high-stakes moments with clear consequences, including physical violence experienced by both Walker and his wife. The Mother's Day testimony alone supplies a complete dramatic arc. The salary-freeze departure adds an unusual internal-organizational conflict not usually narrated in movement memoirs.

universal_themes: First experience of racial exclusion in childhood
Inheriting a parent's posture toward injustice
Conversion of personal anger into disciplined action
Career sacrifice for principle vs family economic need
Long-term marriage tested by political violence
relatable_struggles: Choosing vocation in face of family expectations
Negotiating salary as principal household earner
Carrying physical injuries from years of activism
Reckoning with bitterness in late life
human_moments: Walker's playmate Nancy crying at the curb (Segment 20, 00:02:17,000)
Daddy King looking out the back window at the stream (Segments 411-413, 00:44:52,000)
Theresa Walker refusing to leave the hospital without her children (Segment 594, 00:03:55,000 [01:03:55,000])
Walker saying 'Yes, dear' to his wife (Segment 583, 01:02:18,000)
Walker's discovery that he was born in 1928 not 1929 (Segments 56-58, 00:07:07,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: The childhood-exclusion moment, the marital partnership across danger, the parent-child economic anxiety, and the late-life equanimity offer diverse entry points for audiences without prior civil rights background. The Mother's Day testimony specifically gives a domestic frame to historical violence.
relatable_experiences.justification: Strong universal resonance, especially through Theresa Walker's voice. Walker himself is somewhat more ministerial and elevated in register than a maximally relatable narrator would be, which is why the score does not max out.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: The emotional resonance comes from a combination of Walker's own arc (gun-to-nonviolence; SCLC builder to elder; bitter to reconciled) and Theresa Walker's late-interview testimony, which delivers a complete family-level drama in a few minutes. The personal cost of movement leadership -- broken ribs, dislocated spine, infant children in jail, daughter barred from school -- is rendered with specificity that connects civil rights history to recognizable family experience. The interview's only emotional limitation is Walker's habitual composure, which holds back some of the rawest moments behind a ministerial register.

barriers: Occasional acronyms (SCLC, SNCC, NAACP, ANC) used without definition
Theological references (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John; Buber; Tillich) may require background
Some Whisper artifacts in the transcript ('Father Divine,' 'Mark Adini,' 'Manalovian Church') reflect ASR difficulty more than speaker clarity
language_clarity.justification: Walker speaks clearly and articulately throughout with a measured ministerial cadence. The interviewer helps unpack technical points ('Project C was the blueprint - can you say what that stands for?'). The few acronyms and theological references would be familiar to most U.S. audiences interested in this material.

high_energy_sections: Birmingham Project C reconnaissance and Children's Crusade (Segments 236-275, 00:26:36,000 - 00:30:51,000)
slow_sections: Theological-influence digression (Segments 497-512, 00:54:13,000 - 00:56:13,000) - intellectually substantive but lower momentum
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Sustainable through a 73-minute interview without significant lulls, especially given Theresa Walker's energizing intervention in the final third.
narrative_flow.justification: Generally well-paced with a strong opening, sustained middle through Birmingham, and an energizing final third anchored by Theresa Walker. The theological digression and the brief charter-school passage are the slowest moments but neither derails the flow.

themes_connected: Black-church institutional infrastructure as movement foundation
Continuity of antiracist organizing across U.S. and South Africa
Persistence of residual racism in 'post-racial' framings
Strategic communication and media leverage in social movements
Family as both casualty and partner of movement work
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Walker explicitly rejects the 'post-racial era' framing post-Obama: 'there is so much residual racism that is hardly changed for some people.' His charter-school activism connects to ongoing contemporary education-equity debates, and his anti-apartheid organizing connects U.S. civil rights to global antiracism in ways that remain politically active.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Walker explicitly frames every personal anecdote with its movement meaning: the library sit-in as first Southern desegregation; the Birmingham campaign as what 'desegregated America'; the Letter as 'the most important document of the 20th century'; the apartheid work as continuous with U.S. struggle. He makes the 'so what' connections directly rather than leaving them implicit.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Walker is unusually explicit about historical significance and contemporary relevance. The connections between Petersburg, Birmingham, Harlem, and Johannesburg are drawn at the discourse level rather than left for the listener to assemble. The maximum score reflects how deliberately he scaffolds meaning.

accessibility.overall_assessment: An accessible interview that requires only modest background knowledge. Walker's clear articulation, the interviewer's willingness to ask for definitions, and Walker's habit of explicitly stating historical significance all support broad audience comprehension. The pacing supports sustained attention across 73 minutes, with Theresa Walker's testimony providing energizing variation in the final third. The interview connects personal experience to contemporary issues (post-racial framing; charter schools; global antiracism) without prompting.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is a strong, dense, high-historical-value oral history interview. Walker is a uniquely placed witness to the operational interior of SCLC and the Birmingham campaign, and the interview supplies behind-the-scenes detail that complements rather than duplicates published scholarship. His arc from armed self-defense to nonviolent organizer to long-pastorate elder is articulated through specific incidents rather than abstract claims, and Theresa Walker's testimony in the final third meaningfully elevates the emotional texture. The ceiling on the score comes from Walker's occasional hyperbolic framings (the Letter as 'equal to the Gettysburg address'), some hedged dates that he himself flags, and a ministerial composure that holds back some of the most raw moments. Overall an excellent contribution to the corpus.
key_strengths: Operational insider perspective on SCLC and Project C - Walker designed the Birmingham campaign blueprint, walked downtown reconnaissance, and transcribed the Letter from Birmingham Jail

Verifiable specifics throughout - dates (February 27, 1960; August 16, 1928), budgets ($57,000 to $1,000,000; $10,000 vs $25,000), names (R.G. Williams, Steve Klinsky, Floyd Flake, Robert J. Brown, Maya Angelou), and physical injuries (broken wrist, three ribs, dislocated spine)

Family-level testimony from Theresa Walker that renders movement violence in domestic detail, including the East Point Georgia arrest with five children and the National Guard assault at the Gaston Motel
key_limitations: Walker's hedged or revised dates (1952 meeting King; 1958 Clarksdale; age 22 vs 23 at Petersburg) leave some chronology unresolved within the interview itself - though Walker is transparent about the hedges

Walker's framings are occasionally hyperbolic (the Letter as 'most important document of the 20th century, equals the Gettysburg address') and some claims (FBI infiltration of 'the invaders' in Memphis; first New York charter school) are insider assertions not independently verified in the interview

claims.claim: Walker was born in Brockton, Massachusetts on August 16, 1928
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was born in Brockton, Massachusetts, August 16, 1928 (Segment 4). And later he confirms via his sister finding his birth certificate that his birth year was 1928 (Segment 57).
claims.rationale: Walker states the date directly and corrects an earlier 1929 self-misdating.

claims.claim: Walker grew up the son of a Baptist preacher who served a hundred-member congregation in South Jersey
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father was a congregation in South Jersey of about a hundred small church (Segment 8).

claims.claim: Walker's first playmate Nancy ended the friendship by saying she could not play with him because he was 'colored'
claims.supporting_excerpt: It was a little girl who lived within two blocks from my home, named Nancy... she couldn't play with me anymore. I said why? She said because you're colored (Segments 18-22).

claims.claim: Walker studied physics and chemistry at Virginia Union University and earned an MDiv from its graduate school of religion
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I finished university I was a major in physics and chemistry (Segment 39). Well, I went to the undergraduate school of Virginia Union University and then I went to the graduate school of religion to earn my MDiv (Segment 53).

claims.claim: Walker served Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg for eight years
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was in Petersburg eight years as a pastor at the Gillfield Baptist Church which is over 200 years old (Segment 60).

claims.claim: Walker was 22 (or 23) when called to the Petersburg pastorate
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was 22 years old. I thought I really found out recently that I was 23 (Segment 55).
claims.rationale: Walker states both numbers and explains the discrepancy.

claims.claim: Walker carried a Smith & Wesson nickel-plated .32 in Petersburg before adopting nonviolence under King's influence
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I went to Petersburg as a pastor, I carried a Smith & Wesson nickel-plated .32. And Martin Luther King's influence, I put my gun down (Segments 108-109).

claims.claim: Walker led a Petersburg public library sit-in on February 27, 1960 with R.G. Williams and was arrested
claims.supporting_excerpt: We went there on February 27th, 1960. And we went through the white door, which we were promptly arrested in a few minutes. And two of us, (Rev.) R.G. Williams + I (Walker), the two of us decided to stay in jail (Segments 77-79).

claims.claim: Walker requested volume one of Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee at the library
claims.supporting_excerpt: I asked for a volume one of Douglas Elvall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee (Segment 88).
claims.rationale: Whisper renders 'Southall' as 'Elvall'; the canonical biographer of Lee with that book is Douglas Southall Freeman. Treated as ASR rendering of the same name.

claims.claim: Walker said it was the first library desegregation in the South
claims.supporting_excerpt: It happened that it was the first time the library had been desegregated in the South (Segment 85).
claims.rationale: Direct quotation; the historical-first claim is Walker's own.

claims.claim: Walker met Martin Luther King Jr. in approximately 1952
claims.supporting_excerpt: It must have been 1952. And I joined his organization and he put me on the board (Segments 98-99).
claims.rationale: Walker hedges the date ('It must have been'); the summary repeats the year as Walker stated it but with the same hedge.

claims.claim: Walker joined SCLC at a Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting in 1958
claims.supporting_excerpt: I didn't join the SCLC until the meeting in Clarksdale, Mississippi in 1958, I think it was (Segment 137).
claims.rationale: Walker hedges the year; the summary preserves both the location and the hedged year.

claims.claim: Walker became SCLC Executive Director in 1960, succeeding Ella Baker as interim
claims.supporting_excerpt: And when you came on as a staff member, that was 1960 (Segment 139). He invited me to become Executive Director (Segment 140). She [Ella Baker] was the interim executive director, but I was after her (Segment 145).
claims.rationale: Direct quotations confirm both the year and the succession from Ella Baker.

claims.claim: Walker grew SCLC from a $57,000 first-year budget to a $1,000,000 budget with 100 full-time staff in four years
claims.supporting_excerpt: The budget, my first year was $57,000. We did over 100,000. And in four years... we had a budget of $1,000,000 and a hundred full-time employees (Segments 153-157).

claims.claim: Walker raised Charles Sherrod in his Petersburg congregation and helped finance his education with a Woolworth grant
claims.supporting_excerpt: In fact, I raised him in my congregation in Petersburg. And got a Woolworth grant to help finance his education (Segments 205-206).

claims.claim: Walker authored Project C ('Confrontation') as the Birmingham campaign blueprint and went to Birmingham a year in advance for reconnaissance
claims.supporting_excerpt: Dr. King assigned me to the task of working at a campaign strategy for Birmingham. I went to Birmingham a year ahead of time (Segments 236-237). The Project C confrontation that we had to confront the evil that we discerned in the segregation system (Segment 249).
claims.rationale: Direct quotations establish both the authorship and the C-for-Confrontation naming.

claims.claim: Walker, King, and James Bevel decided to use children in the Birmingham marches
claims.supporting_excerpt: Dr. King and I didn't. And James Bevel and we decided we'd use the children (Segments 264-265).

claims.claim: Bull Connor used fire hoses against children in Birmingham
claims.supporting_excerpt: When people saw the firemen with the [fire] hoses using them against children running them down the sidewalk, they said this won't do (Segments 268-269).

claims.claim: Walker transcribed the Letter from Birmingham Jail as the only person in Birmingham who could read King's chicken-scratch handwriting
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was the only one in Birmingham who could make out Dr. King's chicken scratch writing. And we were doing it from the edges and newspapers, from toilet paper, whatever we had (Segments 377-378).

claims.claim: The Religious Society of Friends (Quakers) first printed the Letter as 'tears of love' before Walker proposed the title 'Letter from the Birmingham Jail'
claims.supporting_excerpt: The Religious Society of Friends, the Quakers, printed the first version of it. And they call it tears of love. I told them, no, it should be called Letter from the Birmingham Jail. So I named it (Segments 394-397).
claims.rationale: Direct quotation; Walker's authorship of the title is his own claim and is directly stated.

claims.claim: Walker was named Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, I was named Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington, which was highly successful (Segments 330-331).

claims.claim: Walker attributes the 1964 Public Accommodations title and the 1965 Voting Rights Act to Birmingham and Selma respectively
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, we attribute to Birmingham, the Public Accommodations Act of 1964, and Birmingham led the Selma, and the Selma March, which led to the Voting Rights Act of 1965 too (Segments 346-347).
claims.rationale: Direct quotation; Walker's attribution chain is preserved as he stated it.

claims.claim: Walker was beaten on Mother's Day with a broken wrist, three broken ribs, and a dislocated back from Alabama State Highway Patrolmen's batons
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I was beaten up on Mother's Day. I had a broken wrist and three broken ribs. And dislocated in my back from the batons of Alabama State Highway Patrolmen (Segments 313-315). When they identified me, they began to beat me three of them. And they broke my wrist, three fractured ribs. And they dislocated my spine with their batons (Segments 615-616).
claims.rationale: Both Walker and his wife independently describe the injuries; consistent.

claims.claim: The SCLC board froze Walker's salary at $10,000 while he administered a million-dollar budget, and a publisher offered $25,000
claims.supporting_excerpt: Oh, honestly, the Board of SCLC froze my salary at $10,000 a year. I had a wife and four children... And this publisher from New York, dangled $25,000 in my face and I was gone (Segments 406-409). And they were paying me, freezing my salary at $10,000 and I had a wife and four children (Segment 422).

claims.claim: Martin Luther King Sr. ('Daddy King') personally visited Walker's Atlanta home to ask him to stay at SCLC
claims.supporting_excerpt: Dr. King had seen me, Rev. Martin Luther King Sr, out to my house in Atlanta to try to persuade me to stay (Segment 411).

claims.claim: Walker pastored a Harlem church for 37 years and grew it to 1,800 members with 80% tithing
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then I went to I was called to a church in Harlem and I served there for 37 years (Segment 63). When I left, you were 1800 members and 80% of them paid tithes (Segment 443).
claims.rationale: Direct quotations. The summary's identification of the Harlem church as 'Canaan Baptist' draws on the ground-truth corpus entry for Walker; the transcript does not name the church, so the summary tags this as corpus-grounded rather than transcript-supported.

claims.claim: Walker was the largest developer of affordable housing in New York State
claims.supporting_excerpt: And at the point that I left New York, I had become the largest developer of affordable housing in New York State (Segment 49).
claims.rationale: Direct quotation; the claim is Walker's own.

claims.claim: Walker opened the first New York charter school with Steve Klinsky and partnered with Floyd Flake and Ruben Diaz Sr. for charter-school legislation
claims.supporting_excerpt: Rubén Díaz Sr, Spanish, passed him in the Bronx, and I led along with Rev. Floyd H. Flake, the struggle to get charter school legislation in the state of New York. And I opened with a fellow named Steve Klinsky. I opened the first charter school in New York (Segments 463-466).

claims.claim: Walker was recruited into the anti-apartheid movement by ANC representatives John Stone and Mark Adini, organized a 300-member church federation, took 22 ministers to South Africa, and participated in Mandela's election
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was recruited by people from the (South) African National Congress, ANC, John Stone, Mark Adini. And so I mobilized churches into a 300-member federation (Segments 474-475). I actually worked in the election of Mandela becoming president (Segment 476). One time I took 22 ministers to be on the ground to see what apartheid was really like (Segment 488).
claims.rationale: All elements directly stated. ANC contact names are Whisper renderings retained verbatim; the summary acknowledges the ASR uncertainty in the chapter errors field.

claims.claim: Theresa Walker was struck in the head by an Alabama National Guardsman at the Gaston Motel on Mother's Day 1963 and later arrested in East Point, Georgia with her five children, with a $84 fine paid by Robert J. Brown
claims.supporting_excerpt: The Alabama National Guardsmen had asked us to go into the lobby of the Gaston Motel (Segment 590). I said I have two children asleep in the motel and the National Guardsman struck me in the head and sent me to the hospital (Segment 593). They arrested my 11-year-old, 10-year-old, the nine-year-old, eight-year-old, and the six-year-old (Segment 600). Robert J. Brown came in and paid my $84.00 fine and got me out of jail (Segment 612).

claims.claim: Theresa Walker served as a Mississippi freedom rider, sharing a two-person cell with 14 other women
claims.supporting_excerpt: And she went to Mississippi as a freedom rider (Segment 617). And she was in our cell for two people with 14 other women (Segment 618).

claims.claim: Walker's daughter was barred from all public schools in Petersburg after the family refused to sign a pupil-placement form and went on to finish Howard University in three years and earn a master's at Yale
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was a pupil placement form that you must sign that you would not send your child to a white school... she was barred from all public schools (Segments 631-633). My daughter finished Howard University in three years. She did a three-year master's at Yale (Segment 636).

claims.claim: Walker's daughter became deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts
claims.supporting_excerpt: She became the deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts (Segment 638).

claims.claim: Maya Angelou worked on Walker's staff running his New York fundraising office on 25th Street and wrote about meeting him with King in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
claims.supporting_excerpt: Maya Angelou used to be on my staff, and she ran our fundraising office in New York on 25th Street (Segment 649). I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings (Segment 652).
claims.rationale: Direct quotations. The specific Angelou quote about being 'too young for wisdom and too handsome for virtue' is Walker quoting from memory; the summary attributes the line to Walker's recollection of Angelou.

claims.claim: Walker says A.D. King's home was bombed in Birmingham, causing community rioting that kept Walker in Birmingham over Mother's Day 1963
claims.supporting_excerpt: Someone had bombed a King, Dr. King's brother's home. And the people were rioting because they had gotten tired of the assaults on the black community (Segments 587-588).
claims.rationale: Direct quotation. The 'King's brother' is A.D. King, confirmed by the ground-truth corpus.

claims.claim: Andrew Young reportedly corrected an author writing on King's intellectual influences, saying King was 'a Christian pastor' influenced 'by Matthew, Mark, Luke and John' rather than by Martin Buber, Henry David Thoreau, and Paul Tillich
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Andrew [Young] said, he had not received that answer from anybody. But more than anything else, Martin Luther King was a Christian pastor (Segments 506-507). Dr. King was influenced by Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John (Segment 505).
claims.rationale: Direct quotation. Walker is recounting Andrew Young's reported statement.

claims.claim: Walker's mentor was Vernon Johns, who taught him that 'the truth is always inflammatory'
claims.supporting_excerpt: My mentor, Dr. Rev. Vernon Johns, you should say that the truth is always inflammatory (Segment 568).

claims.claim: Walker fantasized during college about recruiting young Black men to use plastic bombs against Senators Theodore Bilbo and James Eastland
claims.supporting_excerpt: I envisioned putting together some smart young black man who developed the expertise in using plastic bombs and putting, I speak through the opponent's ears like (Theodore) Bilbo and (James) Eastland (Segments 129-131).

claims.claim: Walker was in Birmingham jail again with King in 1967 for parading without a permit, and took King's photograph there while King took his
claims.supporting_excerpt: In 1967, I was in New York as a pastor and we had a case in Birmingham for parading without a permit (Segment 430). So we went to jail in 1967. And a part of the instrument was he took my picture and I took his picture in the Birmingham jail (Segments 433-434).

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary is dense with verifiable specifics from a uniquely placed primary witness, and the citation audit produced zero unsupported claims across 39 audited factual claims. However, the publication gate fails closed because (1) the main_summary accuracy_score of 86 and quality_score of 88 fall below the 90/90 threshold, primarily due to Walker's own hedged dates (1952 meeting King; 1958 Clarksdale) being repeated faithfully in the summary without external reconciliation, and (2) the citation audit contains two partially_supported claims (Walker's hedged 1952 King meeting; Walker's hedged 1958 SCLC Clarksdale joining), both flagged as partial because Walker himself signals uncertainty in the transcript. The borderline-but-honest scoring routes correctly to human review rather than auto-publishing. A reviewer can decide whether to publish as-is, edit the hedged dates out, or pair the summary with the transcript's date-uncertainty notes.
